role_hints.role: Owner-operators (process-serving firm owners, doc-prep operators, patent agents)
role_hints.oews_basis: role_mix is 4-digit 5411 (Lawyers 62.15%, Paralegals 13.12%) — a law-office mix that does not describe this catch-all code; structure below is hypothesis. 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 2.29% at 4-digit is the only directly relevant line
role_hints.note: Mix of relationship/dispatch management and hands-on production; the production half (document assembly, filings, affidavits) is AI-exposed, the client-relationship half less so.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the 5411 top-10 (often coded 33-9021 Private Detectives or 43-5021 Couriers); hypothesis ~20% of this code's wages
role_hints.note: Physical service of process cannot be done by AI — but it can be abolished by court e-service rules, a regulatory rather than technological substitution. Track both.

role_hints.oews_basis: 23-2011 Paralegals and Legal Assistants 13.12% at 4-digit is mostly law-office paralegals; within this code, doc preparers are plausibly ~20-25% of wages (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: Form completion and filing packets are precisely what generic consumer AI does — the most exposed role in legal services, with no licensure protecting it.

role_hints.role: Patent agents (non-attorney USPTO practitioners) and specialty practitioners
role_hints.note: AI drafts specifications and claims; USPTO registration and prosecution signature stay human.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-xxxx office/admin occupations in the 5411 tail (43-9061 Office Clerks 1.00%, 43-4171 Receptionists 0.73% at 4-digit); hypothesis
role_hints.note: Job routing, affidavit preparation, status tracking — already software tasks in process-serving platforms.

source_hints.source: National Center for State Courts (NCSC) e-filing and court technology resources
source_hints.why: Tracks e-filing/e-service rule adoption — the regulatory substitution channel for process serving.

source_hints.source: LegalZoom and Rocket Lawyer public disclosures (LegalZoom 10-K)
source_hints.why: Consumer doc-prep economics and AI feature rollout — the platform competitors to this code's doc-prep segment.

source_hints.source: NAPPS (National Association of Professional Process Servers) member resources and fee surveys
source_hints.why: Per-serve pricing norms and member counts for the process-serving segment.
Population of non-attorney patent agents — sizes that sub-segment directly.

Sparse micro-deal comps (process-serving and doc-prep businesses); regional process-serving consolidators (e.g. ABC Legal-style platforms) are the only visible roll-up pattern — verify current activity.
Only plausible owner-age evidence for the dominant-by-count segment; expect thin data and say so.

capture_questions: Pricing is per-unit flat fees (per serve, per document packet, per filing) in a fragmented, licensure-free market: when AI cuts the cost of producing a document packet or managing a serve, does per-unit competition pass the saving straight to the client, or do court-integration, e-filing credentials and law-firm dispatch relationships let incumbents hold price?

Can consumers and small businesses go straight to generic AI for the forms and filings that document preparers sell, given no licensure protects the niche — and does UPL enforcement (which both caps what non-lawyers may sell and creates their niche) tighten or loosen as AI legal tools spread?

For process serving, who owns the relationship — law firms, collections agencies and insurers dispatch the work, increasingly via national platforms — and do court e-service rule changes eliminate the product by statute regardless of AI?
Is there any transferable client book at all in the doc-prep segment, or is each transaction one-shot consumer demand won by search/ads (i.e., marketing spend, not a moat)?
terminal_value_question: Legal document preparation is among the most directly AI-substitutable paid services anywhere — no license, no stamp, no physical act protects it. The sharp question: once consumer AI matures, does anything in this code besides physical service of process (itself exposed to e-service rules, not AI) and USPTO-registered patent prosecution retain a reason to be paid — and what share of the code's current revenue do those two protected slivers represent? Score the dominant sub-segment, not the protected edge cases.
special_notes: Catch-all code — identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, and set heterogeneous: true. Hypothesis: (1) legal document preparation (likely dominant by revenue, includes online operations), (2) process serving (largest by establishment count, physical, fragmented), (3) patent agents / notary-adjacent / freelance paralegal services. Their AI exposure and moats differ completely.
Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is the 4-digit 5411 law-office mix (Lawyers 62.15%) and is unrepresentative of this code — build V from the hypothesis structure and flag it as such.

Pricing-model deflation candidate: per-unit flat fees in a zero-licensure market give no structural price floor; assume high pass-through in pi_dist unless evidence of durable dispatch relationships says otherwise.

E-service/e-filing rule changes are an existential regulatory risk to process serving independent of AI — a demand-side statute change, not a technology substitution; keep it out of A but inside terminal value.

Tiny average firm size (4,123 of 4,980 firms under 5 employees; implied EBITDA <$50K in the smallest class) — verify acquirable EBITDA exists outside a few regional process-serving consolidators; n_band 174 is concentrated in the 20-99 and 100-499 classes.

Boundary confusion: law offices are 541110, title abstract/settlement is 541191, notaries are 541120; online legal platforms may report under 541199 or 519/561 codes — check where LegalZoom-style revenue actually sits before sizing sub-segments.
